findings.description: Pagination offset is `page * page_size` but `page` is documented 1-based, so page 1 skips the entire first page; should be `(page - 1) * page_size`.
User-supplied `name_pattern` is compiled and executed as an arbitrary regex with no complexity/timeout guard, enabling catastrophic-backtracking ReDoS that can hang the worker.
`page_size` is only upper-clamped; a zero or negative `page_size` is not validated, yielding an always-empty or surprising slice window.
`page` is never validated for `<= 0`; a negative page produces a negative slice offset and returns results counted from the end of the list.
If `display_name` is NULL in the DB, `matcher.search(None)` (line 60) and `.lower()` (line 62) raise TypeError; null values are not handled.

Entire matching table is fetched, dict-converted, regex-filtered, sorted, and sliced in Python; department filtering, sorting, and pagination should be pushed into SQL (ORDER BY/LIMIT/OFFSET) to bound memory and CPU per request.
`deactivate` returns True via `rowcount == 1` even when the row was already `deleted=1`, so it reports success without any real state change.
A new SQLite connection is opened per call with no reuse/pooling and no busy timeout configured, adding overhead and lock-contention risk under concurrent access.
`SearchResult.page` echoes the raw input `page` even when out of range, so callers cannot distinguish a clamped/valid page from an invalid one.
`department is not None` means an empty-string department becomes a literal `department = ''` filter rather than an unfiltered search, which may be unintended.
Raw exceptions from `_connect`/query execution propagate to the caller with no handling or contextual wrapping in the search path.
Public methods `by_email`, `departments`, and `deactivate` lack docstrings, unlike `search`, reducing API documentation consistency.
